items.front: What governs ATC service in oceanic controlled airspace?
items.back: Use the procedures in Chapter 8 unless a directive or letter of agreement prescribes different procedures or minima.

items.front: What is the ATC sequence for an oceanic weather-deviation request when conflicting traffic exists horizontally?
items.back: Give weather deviations priority, establish vertical separation first, then issue the deviation clearance. If separation cannot be established, advise conflicting traffic and request the pilot's intentions.

items.front: How is ATC service provided in offshore airspace areas?
items.back: Use Chapter 8 procedures and minima, and when Chapter 8 does not cover the situation, apply the rest of the Order.

items.front: When are VFR flight plans permitted in oceanic FIRs?
items.back: Only between sunrise and sunset, only in the listed oceanic areas, and only when basic VFR weather minimums are met.

items.front: What types of separation are approved in Chapter 8?
items.back: Vertical separation, horizontal separation by longitudinal or lateral means, or radar separation where radar coverage is adequate.

items.front: What altimeter setting basis is used for altitude assignment in oceanic control areas?
items.back: Use flight levels and a standard altimeter setting of 29.92 unless directed or charted otherwise.

items.front: What is the oceanic standard altimeter setting?
items.back: 29.92 inches Hg. All altitude assignments are based on flight levels using this standard unless directed or charted otherwise. Per 8-1-5.

items.front: When a position report affecting separation is missing in oceanic operations, how soon must action be taken?
items.back: Take action to obtain the report no later than 10 minutes after the control estimate unless otherwise specified.

items.front: What is the oceanic missing position report deadline?
items.back: 10 minutes after the control estimate — double the domestic 5-minute deadline. Per 8-1-6.

items.front: Why are oceanic gross-navigation, height, time, and SAO verification errors reported?
items.back: They are reported under JO 7210.632 so the data can support risk modeling and separation-standard reduction work.

items.front: What are control estimates used for in oceanic ATC?
items.back: They are time-based estimates of aircraft position, updated by automation or the controller, and they must be used when applying time-based separation minima.

items.front: When may a non-RVSM aircraft be allowed in RVSM oceanic airspace?
items.back: Only if it qualifies as an excepted aircraft and is previously approved by the operations supervisor or CIC.

items.front: What governs ATC service in the North American ICAO Region?
items.back: Use the procedures and minima in Section 8-10.

items.front: How is vertical separation provided in the North American ICAO Region?
items.back: Use Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification plus facility directives that depict the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.

items.front: What distance-based longitudinal minimum may be used in the North American ICAO Region when DME reports or radar observations establish the spacing?
items.back: Use 30 NM; otherwise use at least 40 NM when based on RNAV.

items.front: What lateral minima apply in the North American ICAO Region by navigation approval level?
items.back: Use 50 NM for RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft, 23 NM for approved aircraft in the Anchorage Oceanic and Continental CTAs, and 90 NM for aircraft not covered by those provisions.

items.front: What coordination updates must ARTCCs forward in oceanic operations?
items.back: Forward current flight plan and control information, coordinate in time for receiving-facility action, and forward estimate revisions of 3 minutes or more.

items.front: When is transfer of air-ground communications made when nonradar separation is being applied?
items.back: Make the transfer 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the boundary unless the facilities agree otherwise.

items.front: How is AIDC coordination accomplished where interfacility data communications are implemented?
items.back: Use regional Interface Control Documents supported by letters of agreement between the facilities concerned.

items.front: When two aircraft call for different time-based longitudinal standards, which one is used?
items.back: Apply the larger of the two standards.

items.front: How is reciprocal-track longitudinal separation protected in oceanic operations?
items.back: Keep vertical separation until one of the paragraph's report-based passing conditions is satisfied.

items.front: What is required before reduced longitudinal minima may be used with Mach number technique?
items.back: Use turbojet aircraft only, issue each aircraft a Mach number or approved range, and apply the reduced minima only when the required Mach differential exists.

items.front: What is the Mach number technique?
items.back: Allows reduced longitudinal separation for TURBOJET aircraft only under specified conditions. Per 8-3-3(a).

items.front: What lateral minimum applies within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace between flight paths defined by published Grid System waypoints?
items.back: Use 12 NM.

items.front: What lateral separation in Gulf of America Low Offshore?
items.back: 12 NM between aircraft on published Grid System routes. Per 8-4-1.

items.front: What does the oceanic boundary-crossing method require when an aircraft enters airspace with a larger lateral minimum?
items.back: Use the smaller separation at the boundary and keep at least 15 degrees of divergence until the larger minimum is established.

items.front: At and above FL240, how much route-protected airspace may be retained before widening begins?
items.back: Reduce the protected airspace to 10 miles on each side of centerline out to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then widen on the specified angle.

items.front: What basic divergence is needed for same-NAVAID track separation?
items.back: For VOR, VORTAC, or TACAN radials, use at least 15 degrees of divergence once one aircraft is clear of the other's protected airspace.

items.front: For same-NAVAID VOR/VORTAC/TACAN track separation, what else is required besides at least 15 degrees of divergence?
items.back: One aircraft must be clear of the protected airspace for the other aircraft, using the table distance for the divergence and altitude band.

items.front: In Table 8-4-1, what distance applies to VOR/VORTAC/TACAN tracks diverging 26 to 35 degrees at FL230 and below?
items.back: 11 miles.

items.front: What must controllers protect against when aircraft cross an offshore or oceanic boundary below FL180?
items.back: Ensure differences between QNE and QNH altimeter settings do not compromise vertical separation.

items.front: When does course divergence establish separation as aircraft enter oceanic airspace?
items.back: When the aircraft are on courses diverging by at least 15 degrees, radar separation exists and is increasing at the edge of known radar coverage, and oceanic lateral separation will follow.

items.front: What course divergence provides interim oceanic separation?
items.back: At least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established. Per 8-5-2(a).

items.front: When may an aircraft climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic during transition from offshore to oceanic airspace?
items.back: Only when vertical separation above the opposite-direction traffic is established before boundary crossing and 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.

items.front: Under the same-direction offshore-to-oceanic climb procedure, when must the following aircraft begin climbing?
items.back: It must commence climb within 10 minutes after passing the required reported or radar-observed position of the preceding aircraft.

items.front: When may radar standards be applied before a transitioning aircraft is radar identified?
items.back: Only under the listed transition conditions, and not if there is insufficient time to establish another approved separation method should prompt radar identification fail.

items.front: How may aircraft be separated from a temporary stationary airspace reservation?
items.back: Either laterally, by keeping protected airspace from overlapping the reservation, or vertically, while the aircraft is within the reservation plus the required buffer.

items.front: What if a pilot refuses a clearance to avoid an airspace reservation?
items.back: Advise the pilot of the potential hazard, state that services will not be provided while the flight remains within the reservation, and if possible inform the using agency.

items.front: How may aircraft be separated from a temporary moving airspace reservation?
items.back: Use lateral separation, longitudinal separation ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft in the reservation, or vertical separation with the required buffer.

items.front: What governs ATC service in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: Use the procedures and minima in Section 8-7 except where otherwise noted.

items.front: How is vertical separation provided in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: In accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.

items.front: What reported ITP distance is required between the ITP aircraft and any reference aircraft in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: The reported ITP distance must be 15 NM or more.

items.front: When may 23 NM lateral separation be used in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: Use it for approved aircraft in designated airspace when direct voice or CPDLC communications and the required ADS-C contracts are maintained and monitored by automation.

items.front: What is required for 23 NM North Atlantic lateral separation?
items.back: RNP 4, RCP 240, and RSP 180 at minimum. Per 8-7-4(a).

items.front: What governs ATC service in the Caribbean ICAO Region?
items.back: Use the procedures and minima in Section 8-8 except where otherwise noted.

items.front: How is vertical separation provided in the Caribbean ICAO Region?
items.back: In accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.

items.front: What longitudinal minimum applies to turbojet aircraft operating below FL200 in the listed Caribbean-region areas?
items.back: Use 20 minutes.

items.front: When may 23 NM lateral separation be used in the Caribbean ICAO Region?
items.back: Use it for approved aircraft in designated airspace when direct voice or CPDLC communications and the required ADS-C contracts are maintained and monitored by automation.

items.front: When may IFR flights be cleared to climb or descend in VFR conditions in the Houston, Miami, and San Juan CTAs?
items.back: Only on pilot request, only between sunrise and sunset, and with alternative instructions issued if VFR conditions may become impractical.

items.front: What governs ATC service in the Pacific ICAO Region?
items.back: Use the procedures and minima in Section 8-9 except where otherwise noted.

items.front: How is vertical separation provided in the Pacific ICAO Region?
items.back: In accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.

items.front: What basic time-based longitudinal minima are used in the Pacific ICAO Region before special reductions apply?
items.back: Use 15 minutes between aircraft, or 10 minutes between eligible turbojets on the same or continuously diverging tracks, with Mach technique or ITP used when authorized.

items.front: What lateral minima apply in the Pacific ICAO Region by navigation approval level?
items.back: Use 50 NM for RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft, 23 NM for the approved aircraft in designated 23-NM airspace, and 100 NM for aircraft not covered by those provisions.
